N64233 was approximately 2NM final for the ILS approach to Runway 32R when the pilot reported hitting a bird. They landed safely on Runway 32R and taxied clear on Taxiway A to assess damage. The pilot reported that there was a small dent in the left wing, with no injuries, two personnel on board. Instead of picking up their filed IFR flight plan to ALW, they elected to taxi to parking at Millionair. No other operations were affected. Pilot: Didwell. OWNER: WALLA WALLA UNIV.

ADM Julian took pictures of the aircraft and retrieved blood samples intended to mailed for bird identification at the Smithsonian. N121W LLC. THREE PHOTOS. REMARKS: Pilot reported seeing a possible birdstrike on Runway 16R at 1147 local. Airfield operations responded and discovered remains of a great blue heron at the numbers of 16R. Remains were given to USDA WS biologist for processing. Biologist called tower and there were no reported strikes during this time. Upon reviewing airfield footage, it was found the cameras caught the strike and the tail number of the plane that struck the bird. The pilot was then contacted; they were unaware of the strike. Upon inspection after notification, snarge was found on the leading edge of the wing of the aircraft. Company policy required a mechanic inspection before plane could fly again, there was a one-hour delay to flight. Strike was deemed non damaging by mechanic and flights continued as normal. NOTE: OWNER/ OPERATOR=  BANK OF UTAH TRUSTEE/ Southern Co. Flight.

EFFECT_OTHER: Strike kicked off the auto throttle, crew disconnected auto pilot and hand flew the aircraft to a safe landing.
REMARKS: Talked with the crew and verified the report from ATC. Both the CA and FO reported seeing a medium to large size bird gray in color before impact. They were descending through 5000 feet at 250 kts about 10 miles west of KGEG. Immediatly after impact, the auto throttles kicked off. The pilot flying turned off the autopilot and handflew the aircraft to a safe landing. Snarge collected from nose (Captain side air data probe) and left wing leading edge slat inboard of the engine. QXE MX confirmed there is damage to the air data probe and took the aircraft out of service. Appears to be one bird with multiple strike locations. The bird appears to have struck the nose and then wing. Crew only reported seeing one bird. Between Reardon, WA and Deep Creek, WA. Struck and damaged (unknown scope of damage) CA side air data probe on the nose, struck the nose, and struck the left wing leading edge slat inboard of the No.1 engine. Smithsonian Wildlife/Species Identification: Greater yellowlegs (Tringa melanoleuca) Greater yellowlegs (Tringa melanoleuca)  MOR: QXE2234 was on the downwind at 050 10 miles west/southwest of GEG being vectored for a visual approach when they reported striking what they believed was a bird with the nose of the aircraft. They continued to GEG without incident. Upon inspection of the aircraft, there was no structural damage, but there was damage/obstruction to aircraft instruments. The pilots reported that the bird hit the nose and then closer to the side of the fuselage down to the left wing. The pilots reported that the bird blocked the Captain's side air data computer probe and kicked off auto throttle in flight. The pilots then turned off auto pilot to hand flew the aircraft to the airport.

Delta Maintenance advised ATL Operations of an aircraft (N520DE) that sustained a bird strike parking at Gate A31. Delta Flight DL567, an Airbus A321neo (N520DE), departed SEA at 2242L PDT and arrived ATL at 0537L EDT. During the post-flight inspection, evidence of a bird strike was observed on the radome of the aircraft. The pilot was aware of the strike and had logged it as occurring on takeoff climb (4400') from SEA. ATL Airside Operations collected snarge from the aircraft. According to FlightRadar24, the aircraft departed SEA from RWY 34R. There was visible damage to the radome, requiring replacement; the aircraft was taken out of service.  Per the FAA AC5200-32C this strike (8 miles out from SEA) occurred outside of the FAA critical area (within 5 miles).   Therefore, this strike should not be considered SEA's.A321-271NX, Engine Manufacturer IAE Classification Standard Engine Model PW1133GA-JM. Smithsonian Wildlife/Species Identification: Fox sparrow (Passerella iliaca) SECOND REPORT: Departing SEA at approximately 2245L/1045Z we heard a loud Thump and felt a momentary shudder through the floorboards. We both immediately suspected a bird strike, it was a dark moonless night so we did not see anything. FO was hand flying and didnˇŻt notice or feel any abnormally. I checked all aircraft parameters with extra focus on engine and pressurization, everything was in the normal range. Sent ACARS msg to Dispatch and Maintenance. Made logbook write up and continued flight to destination without incident. Did a post flight walk around and observed a fairly large dent in nose cone below captainˇŻs window. Obvious single large bird strike, probably Seagull. TWO PHOTOS

Large flock of starlings crossed runway during liftoff.  Pilot attempted to climb over flock but flock lifted with the aircraft. Crew reported multiple impacts during gear retraction. A/C returned to BFI as precaution.  No inflight indications of damage/ingestions.  Crew reported aircraft and systems behaved normally.  40-50 carcasses found on RWY.  RWY closed for approx 40 minutes for cleanup. MOR: EJA462 WAS CLEARED FOR TAKEOFF RWY 32L. OFF THE DEPARTURE END OF THE EJA462 REPORTED A BIRD STRIKE AND WANTING TO COMEBACK AND LAND. EJA462 WAS ASKED IF THEY WANTED EMERGENCY SERVICES BUT TURNED THEM DOWN. EJA462 WAS INSTRUCTED TO ENTER THE DOWNWIND, IFR CANCELLATION WAS RECEIVED AND THEY WERE TOLD TO SQUAWK VFR. EJA462 LANDED WITHOUT INCIDENT AND TAXIED TO PARKING. AIRPORT OPS WAS CONTACTED FOR A RWY INSPECTION AND S46 WAS ADVISED OF THE INCIDENT AND CLOSURE OF THE RWY. THE RWY WAS REOPENED AT 2344Z.

Saw a flock of birds in the dark an instant before impact while flying in the downwind for Rwy 15L. Felt the contact and was certain a bird strike had occurred. Did a precautionary landing with no delay (noticed no difference in handling qualities or roughness in engine, but didnˇŻt want to wait to find out). Radioed birdstrike and intention to land promptly, landed without further incident. After landing and stopped with engine off, saw bird/duck lodged in front air filter, a small amount of blood on propeller, and dented left wing leading edge with blood present there too. Flight instructor stated they hit birds 10 ft AGL as they were landing on runway 16L. PAE Ops responded and found two carcasses on the runway 16L landing zone. Snarge was discovered on prop, no damage associated with strike. Remains were given to USDA Wildlife Services biologist for ID. MOR: N6029V HAD BEEN DOING PATTERN WORK ON RWY 16L AND CAME INBOUND TO FULL STOP LAND. AFTER TOUCHDOWN THE PILOT ADVISED THAT THEY HIT ONE OR TWO CROWS AS THEY WERE LANDING. N6029V WAS ABLE TO TAXI TO PARK WITHOUT ISSUE AND THERE WAS NO DAMAGE REPORTED. AIRPORT OPS CONDUCTED A RWY INSPECTION AND FOUND A FEW DEAD BIRDS ON THE RWY.

While performing a touch and go on Runway 36, BGB16 reported striking a small bird short final, possibly multiple small birds. No damage to the aircraft was reported. NOTE: OWNER/ OPERATOR= BIG BEND COMMUNITY COLLEGE.
